article_title: I finally set up Neovim, and it's the terminal editor I didn't know I needed
core_thesis: The author, a long-time nano user, discovers that Neovim fills a valuable middle ground between quick terminal edits (nano) and full IDEs (VS Code). After a deliberate, plugin-by-plugin setup, Neovim becomes his go-to for regular coding tasks due to its portability, extensibility, and efficiency gains from modal editing and plugins like Telescope and Treesitter, despite a non-trivial initial learning curve.
detailed_summary: The article chronicles the author's transition from nano to Neovim. Initially content with nano for quick edits and VS Code for serious work, the author was persuaded by a friend's setup to try Neovim. He already knew basic Vim commands from necessity (e.g., editing on servers without nano), which made the jump easier. He deliberately avoided pre-configured distributions like LazyVim to build his own config from scratch, ensuring he understood every plugin. His essential plugins include lazy.nvim (plugin manager), telescope.nvim (fuzzy finding), nvim-treesitter (syntax highlighting), and the mason/lspconfig trio for language server support. Nice-to-haves include neo-tree.nvim (file explorer), gitsigns.nvim (git diff markers), lualine.nvim (status bar), indent-blankline.nvim (indent guides), and trouble.nvim (diagnostics). The author highlights that Neovim's Lua-first ecosystem and built-in LSP client make it feel modern, and its terminal-native nature allows use over SSH or in tmux. He notes that Neovim scales better than nano for multi-step edits (e.g., using text objects like 'ci"' or visual block mode) and provides superior syntax highlighting via Treesitter. However, he acknowledges friction points: needing a recent Neovim build, inconsistent plugin documentation, the risk of config tweaking becoming a hobby, clipboard complexity over SSH, and unintuitive default keybindings (e.g., Ctrl+V for visual block instead of paste). Despite these, he concludes that Neovim has become his primary editor for regular work, while nano remains for one-off edits on unfamiliar machines.

entities.name: Adam Conway

entities.name: Neovim
entities.role: Terminal-based text editor, the subject of the article.

entities.role: Simple terminal editor the author previously used.

entities.name: VS Code
entities.role: Full-featured IDE the author uses for serious work.

entities.name: telescope.nvim
entities.role: Fuzzy finder plugin for files and codebase search.

entities.name: nvim-treesitter

entities.name: mason.nvim
entities.role: Plugin to install and manage LSP servers and tooling.

key_data_points: The author's essential Neovim setup consists of lazy.nvim, telescope.nvim, nvim-treesitter, and the mason/mason-lspconfig/nvim-lspconfig trio for language server support. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: these plugins provide autocomplete, fuzzy finding, and syntax highlighting, directly improving coding efficiency.)

Neovim's terminal-native nature allows it to work over SSH, inside tmux, or on remote machines where a full IDE cannot be installed. (🟢 Relevant to your DevOps/homelab: you can use the same editor on any server without setup.)

The author deliberately avoided pre-configured distributions (LazyVim, NvChad, etc.) to build his own config from scratch, ensuring he understands every plugin and can debug issues. (🟢 Relevant to your ambition: building your own config gives you full control and deeper learning.)

Neovim's modal editing and text objects (e.g., 'ci"' to change inside quotes, ':%s/old/new/g' for substitutions) enable faster multi-step edits compared to nano. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: these shortcuts can significantly speed up code refactoring.)

Clipboard integration over SSH is non-trivial; the author recommends setting 'vim.opt.clipboard = "unnamedplus"' locally but notes that over SSH the system clipboard refers to the remote machine. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: when working on remote targets, be aware of clipboard limitations.)

The author warns that distro repositories may lag behind Neovim releases, causing cryptic errors with modern plugins. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: always install the latest Neovim from official sources or a PPA.)
methodology_steps: Install a recent version of Neovim (avoid distro repositories; use official binaries or a PPA).
Choose a plugin manager (e.g., lazy.nvim) and install it by following its setup instructions.
Install essential plugins: telescope.nvim (fuzzy finder), nvim-treesitter (syntax highlighting), and the mason/mason-lspconfig/nvim-lspconfig trio for LSP support.
Configure each plugin in your init.lua file. For example, set up telescope with default keymaps, enable treesitter for your languages, and configure mason to automatically install LSP servers.
Add optional quality-of-life plugins: neo-tree.nvim (file explorer), gitsigns.nvim (git diff), lualine.nvim (status bar), indent-blankline.nvim (indent guides), and trouble.nvim (diagnostics).
Set clipboard integration: add 'vim.opt.clipboard = "unnamedplus"' to your config for local use; for SSH, consider using OSC52 or a clipboard forwarding tool.
Remap unintuitive default keybindings (e.g., map Ctrl+V to paste instead of visual block mode) to reduce friction.
Store your entire Neovim configuration in a dotfiles repository for portability across machines.
conclusions: Neovim fills a valuable middle ground between nano and full IDEs, offering efficiency gains for regular coding tasks without replacing either tool entirely. The author now uses Neovim for shell scripts, Python, and config files, while keeping nano for one-off edits on unfamiliar machines.

The initial setup effort is significant but worthwhile for those who will use Neovim regularly. The author emphasizes that building a config from scratch (rather than using a distribution) leads to better understanding and easier debugging.

Neovim's plugin ecosystem, particularly Lua-first plugins and built-in LSP client, makes it feel like a modern editor while remaining terminal-native. This portability is a key advantage for remote work and homelab environments.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to you as a programmer and homelab enthusiast. It provides a practical, step-by-step approach to setting up Neovim from scratch, which you can adapt to your own workflow. The emphasis on building a config you understand aligns with your ambition and desire for control. For Red Teaming, Neovim's terminal-native nature means you can use it over SSH on compromised machines without installing a GUI. The article also highlights efficiency gains from modal editing and plugins like Telescope and Treesitter, which can speed up code editing and navigation. As a student, adopting Neovim could improve your productivity for coding assignments and config management across multiple machines.
extra_advice: Consider creating a minimal Neovim config that works over SSH with no plugins (just built-in LSP and basic keymaps) for quick edits on remote targets. For your homelab, set up a dotfiles repo with a bootstrap script that installs Neovim and your config on any new machine. Also, explore 'vim.keymap.set' to remap Ctrl+V to paste and avoid the visual block mode confusion.
